prompt:
The payments gateway had a brownout last week and our checkout service kept hammering it with doomed requests for 40 minutes, which made the recovery slower for everyone. Classic missing-circuit-breaker. Build package breaker at the root of module go-circuit-breaker, stdlib only.

API I have in mind:
- type Clock interface { Now() time.Time } — injected so the state machine is testable without sleeping
- type State string, constants StateClosed="closed", StateOpen="open", StateHalfOpen="half-open"
- var ErrOpen — sentinel returned for short-circuited calls, errors.Is-able
- New(threshold int, cooldown time.Duration, clock Clock) (*Breaker, error) — threshold >= 1, cooldown > 0, clock non-nil
- (*Breaker).Do(fn func() error) error and (*Breaker).State() State

State machine:
- closed: calls pass through, fn's own error/nil comes straight back. `threshold` CONSECUTIVE failures trip it open — any success resets the streak to zero.
- open: calls fail fast with ErrOpen, fn is never invoked. Once `cooldown` has elapsed (>= boundary counts, measured via the injected clock from the moment it tripped), the breaker is half-open — State() should already report half-open at that point, before any call.
- half-open: exactly ONE trial call goes through; anything else arriving while the trial is in flight gets ErrOpen without invoking fn. Trial succeeds => closed, with a completely fresh failure budget. Trial fails => open again, and the cooldown restarts from the trial failure, not from the original trip.

Checkout calls this from many goroutines concurrently, so every transition has to be race-free, and don't hold your internal lock while the wrapped fn runs in the closed path — some of these calls take 2s.

breaker_test.go is the acceptance suite; leave it as-is. Ship when go test -race -timeout 30s ./... passes.
